news_summary: Recent advancements showcase revolutionary data storage techniques using 3D light and ultra-tiny QR codes, alongside fresh insights refining our understanding of quantum computing progress.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes mettent en avant des techniques révolutionnaires de stockage de données utilisant la lumière 3D et des codes QR ultra-compacts, ainsi que de nouvelles perspectives qui affinent notre compréhension des progrès de l'informatique quantique.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances presentan técnicas revolucionarias de almacenamiento de datos que utilizan luz 3D y códigos QR ultrapequeños, además de nuevos conocimientos que amplían nuestra comprensión de los avances en la computación cuántica.

articles.title: Scientists just found a way to store massive data using light in 3 dimensions
articles.summary: A new holographic storage technique uses light in three dimensions to dramatically increase how much data can be stored. It encodes information throughout a material using amplitude, phase, and polarization, rather than just on a surface. An AI model then reconstructs the data from light patterns, simplifying the process. This could pave the way for faster, denser, and more efficient data storage systems.
articles.content:
Researchers have developed a new holographic data storage method that records and retrieves information in three dimensions by combining three key properties of light -- amplitude, phase and polarization. By using all three together, the approach allows much more data to be stored within the same space, offering a potential solution to the growing global demand for data storage.

Traditional storage systems write data onto flat surfaces such as hard drives or optical discs. In contrast, holographic data storage embeds information throughout the volume of a material using laser light. This creates multiple overlapping light patterns within the same space, which significantly increases storage capacity and enables faster data transfer.

"In conventional holographic data storage, data encoding typically uses one light dimension such as amplitude or phase alone, or, at most, combines two of these dimensions," said research team leader Xiaodi Tan from Fujian Normal University in China. "Based on the principle of polarization holography, we used a deep learning architecture known as a convolutional neural network model to enable the use of polarization as an independent information dimension."

The research, published in Optica, Optica Publishing Group's journal for high-impact research, shows that this new technique can increase how much information is stored while also making it easier to retrieve.

"With further development and commercialization, this type of multidimensional holographic data storage could enable smaller data centers and more efficient large-scale archival storage, while also enhancing data processing and transmission efficiency," said Tan. "It could also contribute to safer data transmission, optical encryption and advanced imaging."

Using Polarization to Expand Data Encoding

In holographic storage, information is saved as image-like data pages created by laser light patterns. Encoding converts digital data into these pages, while decoding translates them back into usable information.

Although light has multiple properties that could be used to carry more data, combining them effectively has been difficult in practice. To overcome this, the researchers refined a method called tensor-based polarization holography, which preserves the polarization state of light during reconstruction. This makes polarization a dependable channel for storing additional information.

Building on this work, the team created a 3D modulation encoding strategy. By adjusting the intensity and phase of two perpendicular polarization states and applying a double-phase hologram technique, they enabled a single phase-only spatial light modulator to encode amplitude, phase and polarization together in the optical field.

AI Decoding of Multidimensional Light Data

Decoding this combined information is challenging because standard sensors only measure light intensity (amplitude) and cannot directly detect phase or polarization. To address this, the researchers used tensor-polarization holography theory along with a convolutional neural network to recover all three types of data from diffraction intensity images.

The neural network is trained using two complementary diffraction images, one captured with a vertical polarizer and one without. By analyzing these images, the model learns to identify patterns linked to amplitude, phase and polarization. This allows it to reconstruct all three simultaneously, improving storage density and boosting data transmission speed.

Toward Faster and Higher-Capacity Data Storage

After confirming the concept, the researchers built a compact system capable of recording and reconstructing the encoded optical field within a polarization-sensitive material. During testing, intensity images were analyzed to detect signatures related to amplitude, phase and polarization. These were then used as inputs for the neural network, enabling full 3D reconstruction using only intensity-based measurements.

"Overall, our results showed that multidimensional joint encoding substantially increased the information carried by a single holographic data page, thereby improving storage capacity," said Tan. "In addition, neural network synchronous decoding reduced the need for complex measurements and step-by-step reconstruction, supporting more efficient readout and decoding. This could enable a practical route toward high-capacity, high-throughput holographic data storage."

Next Steps for Real-World Applications

The researchers emphasize that the system is still in the research stage and requires further development before it can be used commercially. Future work will focus on increasing the gray levels used in encoding to expand capacity even further, as well as improving the long-term stability, uniformity and repeatability of the recording materials.

They also plan to integrate this method with volumetric holographic multiplexing techniques, which could allow multiple pages and channels of data to be stored at once. Strengthening the integration between optical hardware and decoding algorithms will be essential for achieving faster and more reliable data retrieval under real-world conditions.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant breakthrough in data storage technology using multidimensional holographic techniques combined with AI decoding, which can substantially increase storage capacity and efficiency. This innovation addresses the growing global demand for data storage, has broad potential applications including smaller data centers and safer data transmission, and is supported by detailed research and experimental validation.
articles.personality_title: Scientists develop 3D light-based method to store much more data
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As the world creates more and more digital information, we need better ways to store all that data. Traditional storage devices like hard drives save data on flat surfaces, but they have limits on how much they can hold.

**What happened** – Scientists at Fujian Normal University in China invented a new way to store data using light in three dimensions. They use three properties of light—amplitude, phase, and polarization—to encode information inside a material, not just on its surface. An artificial intelligence (AI) model then reads the data by analyzing patterns of light, making the process simpler and more efficient.

**Impact** – This method can store much more data in the same amount of space compared to older systems. It also speeds up how fast data can be saved and retrieved. This is important because it could lead to smaller data centers that use less energy and better ways to send data securely. Unlike previous methods that used only one or two light properties, this technique uses all three, unlocking a new level of storage capacity.

**What's next step** – The researchers will work on making the system more stable and able to store even more detailed information. They also want to combine this technique with other holographic methods to store multiple data pages at once. Improving the connection between the hardware (the devices that record and read the data) and the AI decoding will help make this technology ready for real-world use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By using light’s full properties and AI, scientists have created a new 3D data storage method that can hold much more information in less space and read it faster.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques développent une méthode 3D utilisant la lumière pour stocker beaucoup plus de données
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Avec la croissance rapide des informations numériques, il est nécessaire de trouver de meilleures façons de stocker toutes ces données. Les dispositifs traditionnels comme les disques durs enregistrent les données sur des surfaces plates, mais leur capacité est limitée.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université Normale du Fujian en Chine ont inventé une nouvelle méthode pour stocker des données en utilisant la lumière en trois dimensions. Ils utilisent trois propriétés de la lumière — l’amplitude, la phase et la polarisation — pour coder l’information à l’intérieur d’un matériau, pas seulement sur sa surface. Un modèle d’intelligence artificielle (IA) lit ensuite les données en analysant les motifs lumineux, rendant le processus plus simple et efficace.

**Impact** – Cette méthode peut stocker beaucoup plus de données dans le même espace que les systèmes anciens. Elle accélère aussi la vitesse d’enregistrement et de lecture des données. Cela peut permettre d’avoir des centres de données plus petits et moins gourmands en énergie, ainsi que des moyens plus sûrs de transmettre des informations. Contrairement aux méthodes précédentes qui utilisaient une ou deux propriétés de la lumière, cette technique exploite les trois, offrant une capacité de stockage nouvelle.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont travailler à rendre le système plus stable et capable de stocker des informations encore plus détaillées. Ils souhaitent aussi combiner cette technique avec d’autres méthodes holographiques pour stocker plusieurs pages de données en même temps. Améliorer la connexion entre le matériel et le décodage par IA aidera à rendre cette technologie utilisable dans la vie réelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En utilisant toutes les propriétés de la lumière et l’IA, des scientifiques ont créé une nouvelle méthode de stockage 3D qui peut contenir beaucoup plus d’informations dans moins d’espace et les lire plus rapidement.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos desarrollan método 3D con luz para almacenar mucha más información
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que el mundo genera más información digital, necesitamos mejores formas de almacenarla. Los dispositivos tradicionales como los discos duros guardan datos en superficies planas, pero tienen un límite en la cantidad que pueden contener.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Normal de Fujian en China inventaron una nueva forma de almacenar datos usando luz en tres dimensiones. Usan tres propiedades de la luz — amplitud, fase y polarización — para codificar información dentro de un material, no solo en su superficie. Un modelo de inteligencia artificial (IA) luego lee los datos analizando patrones de luz, haciendo el proceso más sencillo y eficiente.

**Impacto** – Este método puede almacenar mucha más información en el mismo espacio que los sistemas antiguos. También acelera la velocidad para guardar y recuperar datos. Esto podría permitir centros de datos más pequeños que usen menos energía y formas más seguras de enviar datos. A diferencia de métodos anteriores que usaban una o dos propiedades de la luz, esta técnica usa las tres, aumentando mucho la capacidad de almacenamiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajarán para hacer el sistema más estable y capaz de guardar información aún más detallada. También quieren combinar esta técnica con otros métodos holográficos para almacenar varias páginas de datos al mismo tiempo. Mejorar la conexión entre el hardware y el decodificador de IA ayudará a que esta tecnología pueda usarse en la vida real.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando todas las propiedades de la luz y la IA, científicos crearon un nuevo método de almacenamiento 3D que puede guardar mucha más información en menos espacio y leerla más rápido.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, translucent cube filled with intricate, overlapping 3D light waves in soft blues and purples, each wave displaying distinct patterns representing amplitude, phase, and polarization, surrounded by gentle swirling neural network-like lines connecting the cube to a radiant light source above, all rendered in natural, muted colors on a simple, dark background.

articles.title: World's smallest QR code, smaller than bacteria, could store data for centuries
articles.summary: Scientists have created a microscopic QR code so tiny it can only be seen with an electron microscope—smaller than most bacteria and now officially a world record. But this isn’t just about size; it’s about durability. By engraving data into ultra-stable ceramic materials, the team has opened the door to storing information that could last for centuries or even millennia without needing power or maintenance.
articles.content:
How small can a QR code get? A team of researchers has pushed the limits to an extreme, creating one so tiny it can only be detected using an electron microscope. Scientists at TU Wien, working with data storage company Cerabyte, produced a QR code measuring just 1.98 square micrometers, which is smaller than most bacteria. This achievement has now been officially confirmed and recorded in the Guinness Book of Records.

Beyond its size, the breakthrough could have major implications for long-term data storage. Traditional storage technologies such as magnetic drives or electronic systems tend to degrade within a few years. In contrast, encoding information into ceramic materials could preserve it for hundreds or even thousands of years.

Stable and Readable at the Nanoscale

"The structure we have created here is so fine that it cannot be seen with optical microscopes at all," says Prof. Paul Mayrhofer from TU Wien's Institute of Materials Science and Technology. "But that is not even the truly remarkable part. Structures on the micrometer scale are nothing unusual today -- it is even possible to fabricate patterns made of individual atoms. However, that alone does not result in a stable, readable code."

At extremely small scales, atoms can shift positions or fill gaps, which can erase stored data. "What we have done is something fundamentally different," Mayrhofer explains. "We have created a tiny, but stable and repeatedly readable QR code."

Ceramic Materials Enable Durable Data Storage

The key to this achievement lies in the material itself. "We conduct research on thin ceramic films, such as those used for coating high-performance cutting tools," explain Erwin Peck and Balint Hajas. "For high-performance tools, it is essential that materials remain stable and durable even under extreme conditions. And that is exactly what makes these materials ideal for data storage as well."

Using focused ion beams, the researchers engraved the QR code into a thin ceramic layer. Each pixel measures just 49 nanometers, which is about ten times smaller than the wavelength of visible light. As a result, the pattern is completely invisible under normal conditions and cannot be resolved using visible light. However, when viewed with an electron microscope, the QR code can be clearly and reliably read.

The storage capacity is also impressive. More than 2 terabytes of data could fit within the area of a single A4 sheet of paper using this approach. Unlike conventional storage systems, these ceramic data carriers can remain intact indefinitely and do not require any energy to maintain the stored information.

A New Approach to Long-Term Data Preservation

"We live in the information age, yet we store our knowledge in media that are astonishingly short-lived," says Alexander Kirnbauer. Magnetic and electronic storage devices often lose data after only a few years, especially without continuous power, cooling, and maintenance. In contrast, ancient civilizations carved their knowledge into stone, allowing it to survive for thousands of years.

"With ceramic storage media, we are pursuing a similar approach to that of ancient cultures, whose inscriptions we can still read today," Kirnbauer says. "We write information into stable, inert materials that can withstand the passage of time and remain fully accessible to future generations."

Another major advantage is energy efficiency. Unlike modern data centers that require significant electricity and cooling, ceramic-based storage can preserve information without any ongoing energy input, helping reduce environmental impact.

Guinness Record and Future Applications

The record-setting QR code and its verification process, including electron microscope readout, were conducted jointly by TU Wien and Cerabyte in front of witnesses. The University of Vienna served as an independent verifier. TU Wien provided advanced materials science facilities along with the high-resolution electron microscopes at its USTEM center. The result has now been officially recognized by Guinness, with the new QR code measuring just 37% the size of the previous record holder.

"The now confirmed world record marks just the beginning of a very promising development," says Alexander Kirnbauer. "We now aim to use other materials, increase writing speeds, and develop scalable manufacturing processes so that ceramic data storage can be used not only in laboratories but also in industrial applications. At the same time, we are investigating how more complex data structures -- far beyond simple QR codes -- can be written robustly, quickly, and energy-efficiently into ceramic thin films and read out reliably."

This work points toward a more sustainable future for data storage, where information can be preserved securely for the long term with minimal energy use.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in ultra-durable, energy-efficient data storage using microscopic ceramic QR codes. This innovation has broad implications for long-term data preservation, potentially benefiting society by enabling secure, sustainable information storage for centuries without power or maintenance. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a verified world record and future scalable applications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create world's smallest QR code that could store data for centuries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Researchers have been looking for new ways to store data that last longer than current methods. Traditional storage like hard drives can break down after a few years, so scientists want something more durable that doesn’t need electricity to keep information safe.

**What happened** – A team from TU Wien and Cerabyte made the smallest QR code ever, measuring only 1.98 square micrometers—smaller than most bacteria. This tiny code can only be seen with an electron microscope. They engraved it onto a thin layer of special ceramic using focused ion beams. This material is very stable and can hold data without changing for hundreds or even thousands of years.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows data can be stored in a way that lasts much longer than current technologies. The ceramic QR code doesn’t need power or maintenance to keep the information safe. Also, the method can fit a huge amount of data—more than 2 terabytes—on something as small as a sheet of paper. This could help us save important information for future generations in a very durable and energy-efficient way.

**What's next step** – The researchers want to improve this technology by using different materials, speeding up how fast they can write data, and making it easier to produce on a larger scale. They also plan to store more complex types of data, not just QR codes, and hope to bring this method into real-world industrial use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have made the world’s smallest, most durable QR code using ceramic materials, opening the door to storing large amounts of data safely for centuries without energy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent le plus petit code QR au monde capable de stocker des données pendant des siècles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chercheurs cherchent de nouvelles façons de stocker les données plus longtemps que les méthodes actuelles. Les supports traditionnels comme les disques durs peuvent tomber en panne après quelques années, donc ils veulent quelque chose de plus durable qui ne nécessite pas d’électricité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de TU Wien et Cerabyte a créé le plus petit code QR jamais réalisé, mesurant seulement 1,98 micromètres carrés, plus petit que la plupart des bactéries. Ce code minuscule ne peut être vu qu’avec un microscope électronique. Ils l’ont gravé sur une fine couche de céramique spéciale utilisant des faisceaux d’ions focalisés. Ce matériau est très stable et peut conserver les données sans changer pendant des centaines voire des milliers d’années.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que les données peuvent être stockées de manière beaucoup plus durable que les technologies actuelles. Le code QR en céramique ne nécessite ni énergie ni entretien pour garder les informations sûres. De plus, cette méthode peut contenir une énorme quantité de données—plus de 2 téraoctets—sur une surface aussi petite qu’une feuille de papier. Cela pourrait aider à préserver des informations importantes pour les générations futures de façon durable et économe en énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer cette technologie en utilisant différents matériaux, en accélérant la vitesse d’écriture des données et en facilitant la production à grande échelle. Ils prévoient aussi de stocker des données plus complexes, pas seulement des codes QR, et espèrent utiliser cette méthode dans l’industrie.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont créé le plus petit et plus durable code QR en céramique, ouvrant la voie au stockage sûr de grandes quantités de données pendant des siècles sans énergie.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean el código QR más pequeño del mundo que podría almacenar datos por siglos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los investigadores buscan nuevas formas de almacenar datos que duren más que los métodos actuales. Los dispositivos tradicionales como los discos duros pueden fallar después de algunos años, por eso quieren algo más duradero que no necesite electricidad.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de TU Wien y Cerabyte creó el código QR más pequeño del mundo, midiendo solo 1,98 micrómetros cuadrados, más pequeño que la mayoría de las bacterias. Este código tan pequeño solo puede verse con un microscopio electrónico. Lo grabaron en una fina capa de cerámica especial usando haces de iones enfocados. Este material es muy estable y puede guardar datos sin cambiar durante cientos o incluso miles de años.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que los datos pueden almacenarse de forma mucho más duradera que las tecnologías actuales. El código QR de cerámica no necesita energía ni mantenimiento para mantener la información segura. Además, este método puede contener una gran cantidad de datos—más de 2 terabytes—en un área tan pequeña como una hoja de papel. Esto podría ayudar a guardar información importante para futuras generaciones de manera duradera y eficiente en energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren mejorar esta tecnología usando diferentes materiales, aumentando la velocidad con que escriben los datos y facilitando la producción a gran escala. También planean almacenar datos más complejos, no solo códigos QR, y esperan que este método se use en la industria.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos han creado el código QR más pequeño y duradero usando cerámica, abriendo la puerta para almacenar grandes cantidades de datos de forma segura durante siglos sin energía.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a tiny, glowing ceramic tile etched with a delicate, intricate QR code pattern visible only under a softly glowing electron microscope beam, resting on a textured surface resembling ancient stone tablets, symbolizing the fusion of cutting-edge nanotechnology and timeless data preservation.

articles.title: This quantum computing breakthrough may not be what it seemed
articles.summary: A team of physicists set out to test some of the most exciting claims in quantum computing—and found a very different story. Instead of confirming breakthroughs, their careful replication studies revealed that signals once hailed as major advances could actually be explained in simpler ways. Despite the importance of these findings, their work initially struggled to get published, highlighting a deeper issue in science.
articles.content:
A team of researchers led by Sergey Frolov, a physics professor at the University of Pittsburgh, along with collaborators from Minnesota and Grenoble, carried out a series of replication studies focused on topological effects in nanoscale superconducting and semiconducting devices. This area of research is considered crucial because it could enable topological quantum computing, a proposed approach to storing and processing quantum information in a way that naturally resists errors.

Across multiple experiments, the researchers consistently identified other ways to interpret the same data. Earlier studies had presented these results as major steps forward in quantum computing and were published in leading scientific journals. However, the follow-up replication studies struggled to gain acceptance from those same journals. Editors often rejected them on the grounds that replication work lacks novelty or that the field had already moved on after a few years. In reality, replication studies require significant time, resources, and careful experimentation, and meaningful scientific questions do not become outdated so quickly.

Combining Evidence and Calling for Reform

To strengthen their case, the researchers brought together several replication efforts into a single, comprehensive paper focused on topological quantum computing. Their goal was twofold: to show that even striking experimental signals that appear to confirm major breakthroughs can sometimes be explained in other ways, especially when more complete datasets are analyzed, and to suggest improvements to how research is conducted and reviewed. These proposed changes include greater data sharing and more open discussion of alternative interpretations to improve the reliability of experimental findings.

A Lengthy Path to Publication

Gaining acceptance for these conclusions took time. The broader scientific community needed extensive discussion and debate before considering the possibility that earlier interpretations might be incomplete. The paper underwent a record two years of peer and editorial review after being submitted in September 2023. It was ultimately published in the journal Science on January 8, 2026.

A group of scientists, including Sergey Frolov, professor of physics at the University of Pittsburgh, and coauthors from Minnesota and Grenoble have undertaken several replication studies centered around topological effects in nanoscale superconducting or semiconducting devices. This field is important because it can bring about topological quantum computing, a hypothetical way of storing and manipulating quantum information while protecting it against errors.

In all cases they found alternative explanations of similar data. While the original papers claimed advances for quantum computing and made their way into top scientific journals, the individual follow-ups could not make it past the editors at those same journals. Reasons given for its rejection included that being a replication it was not novel; that after a couple of years the field has moved on. But replications take time and effort and the experiments are resource-intensive and cannot happen overnight. And important science does not become irrelevant on the scale of years.

The scientists then united several replication attempts in the same field of topological quantum computing into a single paper. The aim was twofold: demonstrate that even very dramatic signatures that may appear consistent with major breakthroughs can have other explanations-especially when fuller datasets are considered, and outline changes to the research and peer review process that have the potential to increase the reliability of experimental results: sharing more data and openly discussing alternative explanations.

It took significant time and argumentation for the rest of the community to accept this possibility: the paper spent a record two years under peer and editorial review. It was submitted in September 2023. It was published in the journal Science on January 8, 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific development where replication studies challenge previously claimed quantum computing breakthroughs, promoting greater scientific rigor and reliability. This has broad implications for the future of quantum computing technology and scientific research practices, benefiting the general public by fostering more trustworthy advancements.
articles.personality_title: New study questions past quantum computing breakthroughs through careful replication
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computing is a new technology that could change how computers work by using tiny particles to store and process information. Scientists have been excited about certain discoveries in this field, especially related to something called topological quantum computing, which might help computers avoid errors.

**What happened** – A group of physicists, led by Sergey Frolov from the University of Pittsburgh, repeated several important experiments in this area to see if the results were correct. They found that the data could be explained in simpler ways, not just the exciting breakthroughs originally claimed. Their replication studies took a long time and were hard to get published because journals said such work was not new or that the field had moved on.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it shows how science can improve by checking past results carefully. It also highlights problems in how scientific studies are published, especially when replication work is undervalued. By combining several replication studies into one report, the team suggests better ways to share data and discuss different ideas openly, which can make future research more reliable.

**What's next step** – The paper was finally published in the journal Science after two years of review. This could lead to more scientists repeating experiments and sharing their data openly. It might also encourage journals to accept replication studies more often, helping science become more trustworthy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Careful repetition of key quantum computing experiments reveals simpler explanations and calls for more open and reliable scientific research practices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude remet en question les avancées passées en informatique quantique grâce à des réplications rigoureuses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'informatique quantique est une technologie nouvelle qui pourrait transformer le fonctionnement des ordinateurs en utilisant de très petites particules pour stocker et traiter des informations. Les scientifiques étaient enthousiastes à propos de certaines découvertes, notamment en lien avec l'informatique quantique topologique, qui pourrait aider les ordinateurs à éviter les erreurs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe de physiciens, dirigé par Sergey Frolov de l'Université de Pittsburgh, a répété plusieurs expériences importantes dans ce domaine pour vérifier leurs résultats. Ils ont découvert que les données pouvaient être expliquées de manière plus simple, et pas seulement par les avancées prometteuses initialement annoncées. Ces études de réplication ont pris beaucoup de temps et ont eu du mal à être publiées car les revues estimaient que ce travail n'était pas nouveau ou que le domaine avait évolué.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre comment la science peut s'améliorer en vérifiant soigneusement les résultats passés. Elle met aussi en lumière des problèmes dans la publication scientifique, surtout quand les travaux de réplication sont sous-estimés. En combinant plusieurs études de réplication dans un seul rapport, l'équipe propose de meilleures façons de partager les données et de discuter ouvertement des idées différentes, ce qui rendra la recherche future plus fiable.

**Prochaine étape** – L'article a finalement été publié dans la revue Science après deux ans d'examen. Cela pourrait encourager davantage de scientifiques à répéter les expériences et à partager leurs données ouvertement. Cela pourrait aussi pousser les revues à accepter plus souvent les études de réplication, renforçant ainsi la confiance dans la science.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La répétition attentive d'expériences clés en informatique quantique révèle des explications plus simples et appelle à des pratiques de recherche plus ouvertes et fiables.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio cuestiona avances pasados en computación cuántica mediante replicaciones cuidadosas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La computación cuántica es una tecnología nueva que podría cambiar la forma en que funcionan las computadoras usando partículas muy pequeñas para almacenar y procesar información. Los científicos estaban emocionados por ciertos descubrimientos, especialmente relacionados con la computación cuántica topológica, que podría ayudar a las computadoras a evitar errores.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de físicos, liderado por Sergey Frolov de la Universidad de Pittsburgh, repitió varios experimentos importantes en este campo para ver si los resultados eran correctos. Encontraron que los datos podían explicarse de maneras más simples, no solo con los avances emocionantes que se habían anunciado originalmente. Estos estudios de replicación tomaron mucho tiempo y fue difícil publicarlos porque las revistas dijeron que este trabajo no era nuevo o que el campo ya había avanzado.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra cómo la ciencia puede mejorar revisando cuidadosamente resultados anteriores. También destaca problemas en cómo se publican los estudios científicos, especialmente cuando se subestima el trabajo de replicación. Al combinar varios estudios de replicación en un solo informe, el equipo sugiere mejores formas de compartir datos y discutir ideas diferentes abiertamente, lo que puede hacer que la investigación futura sea más confiable.

**Próximo paso** – El artículo fue finalmente publicado en la revista Science después de dos años de revisión. Esto podría llevar a que más científicos repitan experimentos y compartan sus datos abiertamente. También podría animar a las revistas a aceptar más estudios de replicación, ayudando a que la ciencia sea más confiable.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La repetición cuidadosa de experimentos clave en computación cuántica revela explicaciones más simples y llama a prácticas de investigación más abiertas y confiables.
